ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Chicken Tikka Burger; closest defensible match is chicken sandwich food product (FOODON_00002473). Tikka spicing and curry mayo are not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FAN covers chicken burger/sandwich category. Tikka marinade and curry mayo specifics are not represented; closest_parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chicken Tikka Burger as a food concept; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: tikka marinade (yogurt, turmeric, cumin, coriander, chilli, ginger, garlic)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising one assembled chicken tikka burger (bun, tikka chicken patty, curry mayo, lettuce, tomato, onion) plus a side of french fries.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Tikka Burger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Tikka Burger (cr_id G00010652). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Hamburger / burger bun analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Tikka Burger. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle being a refined wheat flour burger bun, which is analogous to white bread / burger bun entries (GI ~65–70) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Tikka chicken and curry mayo contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 29.57 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 47.9 for a 350 g serving (carbohydrate contribution ~73.6 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 487 mg/100 g with low potassium offset (197 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: At 487 mg sodium per 100 g and a sodium-to-potassium ratio of approximately 2.5:1, this dish poses a high concern for hypertensive individuals; portion size should be strictly limited and lower-sodium preparation (reduced marinade salt, low-sodium mayo) is strongly recommended.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined-flour bun driving 29.6 g carbohydrate/100 g with only 1.8 g dietary fibre/100 g and estimated GI of 65
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined wheat bun contributes a medium-to-high glycaemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute a high-fibre wholegrain bun, reduce portion size, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 10.18 g/100 g, sodium 487 mg/100 g, cholesterol 29 mg/100 g; saturated fat breakdown not reported
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of moderate total fat, elevated sodium, and an unquantified saturated fat fraction warrants caution for cardiovascular disease patients; grilled tikka chicken itself is a lean protein source, but the bun, curry mayo, and fries collectively elevate the atherogenic and sodium burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Tikka Burger is an Indian-fusion fast-food dish popular in Singapore, consisting of a grilled or pan-seared chicken tikka patty — marinated in a spiced yogurt blend of turmeric, cumin, coriander, chilli, ginger, and garlic — served in a refined wheat flour burger bun with homemade curry mayonnaise, fresh lettuce, sliced tomato, and onion rings, accompanied by a side of french fries. The dish delivers 263 kcal per 100 g, with 13.3 g protein, 10.18 g fat, and 29.57 g carbohydrate. The tikka chicken component is a lean, high-protein element with anti-inflammatory spice notes, while the refined bun and curry mayo drive the carbohydrate and fat load. Sodium is elevated at 487 mg/100 g, making this dish a moderate-to-high concern for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The estimated glycaemic index of 65 (medium) reflects the refined bun as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle. Clinically, the dish is not recommended for Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension without modification; substituting a wholegrain bun and reducing marinade salt would substantially improve its nutritional profile. The dish is halal-suitable and is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore's Indian-fusion food scene.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chicken tikka burger?
Is chicken tikka burger suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010652) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on the refined wheat bun as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Chicken Tikka Burger is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010652) as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient fields are not reported in the HPB source for this dish. potassium_mg (197), calcium_mg (116), iron_mg (2.87), magnesium_mg (22), cholesterol_mg (29) are populated from the HPB source. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat burger bun entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Chicken Tikka Burger was found in any approved source. Halal status is inferred from the Indian-Muslim food context and tikka preparation norms in Singapore; formal halal certification should be verified with the specific vendor. The input record listed 'diabetes' and 'rheumatoid_arthritis' as health suitability conditions; these have been mapped to 'type2_diabetes' and incorporated into health_context per Veritas naming rules. Rheumatoid arthritis is captured as an additional_conditions note in the source input but is not a mandatory Veritas health_context condition and has not been added to the structured health_context block.
